coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-guided luminous microsphere for anchoring and lightening Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Han
Zhang, Xu
Yao, Mingru
Yang, Qingli
Wu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Aptamers are recognition elements that are easy to synthesize and store, but their softness makes their stability and affinity subject to the influence of the environment. Herein, by taking advantage of the aptamer-specific ability to recognize targets and the fluorescence of nontoxic carbon dots (CDs), we constructed aptamer-guided luminous microspheres (~200 nm in size). These microspheres, with tetrahedral DNA (Td) as the skeleton, can actively recognize Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium (S. Typhimurium) and fluoresce after sensitively binding to the target. The fluorescence intensity emitted by the microspheres increased 3.05 times. The limitation of detection was 9 CFU/mL, with a detection range of 10–108 CFU/mL, and the recovery rate in qualified pasteurized milk and drinking water samples was 95.35–103.01%. Additionally, this presented fluorescence signal amplification strategy provides novel insights into the analysis of various food threat factors and other fluorescence imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA tetrahedron
Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium
Aptamer
Carbon dot
Sandwich biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle and establishment of the sandwich fluorescence detection platform
Synthesis and characterization of Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres
Analytical performance of the sandwich fluorescence assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team (2019) , Qingdao Science and Technology Fund ( 21-1-4-sf-6-nsh ), the National Key Research and Development Program under Grant No. 2016YFD0400105 , and High-level Talent Start-up fund from Qingdao Agricultural University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra collected from the CD421; (b) UV/Vis absorption (Inset: photo of CD421 solution in daylight and under 365 nm lamp); (c) AFM image (Inset: size distribution of CD421); (d) TEM image of CD421 (Inset: HRTEM image of CD421).

(a) 3% agarose gel electrophoresis result for Td-Apt: lane 1: DNA Marker, lane 2: A strand, lane 3: AB, lane 4: ABC, lane 5: Td, lane 6: Td-Apt; (b) AFM images of Td-Apt; (c) LSCM results of S. Typhimurium captured by aptamer (left) and Td-Apt (right) dyed with FAM fluorescent dye, overlay chart of FCM results (d) (pink: Apt-FAM; blue: Td-Apt-FAM), and the mean fluorescence intensity of FCM of the two (e).

(a) AFM photo of Td-CD421 microspheres; (b) Fluorescent spectrum of S. Typhimurium (1.0 × 106 CFU/mL) detected by Td-CD421 microspheres (dark blue line), S. Typhimurium cells (1.0 × 106 CFU/mL) detected by SA-CD421 (red line), and blank control detected by Td-CD421 microspheres (gray line).

Td-CD421 microsphere fluorescence signal amplification. (a) LSCM pictures of aptamer-CD421 (left) and Td-CD421 microsphere (right) capturing S. Typhimurium, overlay chart of FCM results (b) (pink: Apt-CD421; green: Td-CD421), and the mean fluorescence intensity of FCM of the two (c). Fluorescence spectra (d) and the fluorescence value at 421 nm (e) of CD421 in different states (pure CD421, SA-CD421, microspheres and biotin~SA-CD421).

Fluorescent responses of the developed biosensor toward varied concentration of bacteria cell. (a) The fluorescence spectra of the detection system challenged with blank control and 10–1.0 × 108 CFU/mL of S. Typhimurium cells; (b) Calibration curve for S. Typhimurium detection with blank control and 10–108 CFU/mL. The linear regression equation was fluorescence intensity= 75.754ln(x)+ 303.75, and the R2 = 0.9941; (c) Fluorescence detection results: The concentration of S. Typhimurium ranged from low to high (left to right); (d) The fluorescence intensity detected by different bacteria (V. parahaemolyticus, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. Typhimurium.) at the same concentration of 108 CFU/mL.

Schematic illustration of this work. (a) Assembly process of Td-Apt structure, signal recognition element and signal amplification element, (b) Determination process using the sandwich fluorescence detection biosensor.
Comparison of detection strategies for S. Typhimurium with the reported methods.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-guided luminous microsphere for anchoring and lightening Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, 271018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Crop Biology, College of Horticulture Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crop Biology, College of Horticulture Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Crop Biology, College of Horticulture Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, 271018, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aptamers are recognition elements that are easy to synthesize and store, but their softness makes their stability and affinity subject to the influence of the environment. Herein, by taking advantage of the aptamer-specific ability to recognize targets and the fluorescence of nontoxic carbon dots (CDs), we constructed aptamer-guided luminous microspheres (~200 nm in size). These microspheres, with tetrahedral DNA (Td) as the skeleton, can actively recognize Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium (S. Typhimurium) and fluoresce after sensitively binding to the target. The fluorescence intensity emitted by the microspheres increased 3.05 times. The limitation of detection was 9 CFU/mL, with a detection range of 10–108 CFU/mL, and the recovery rate in qualified pasteurized milk and drinking water samples was 95.35–103.01%. Additionally, this presented fluorescence signal amplification strategy provides novel insights into the analysis of various food threat factors and other fluorescence imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogens are a serious hazard to food safety worldwide; they have a wide distribution and result in substantial pollution and serious harm because of their small size and short propagation period [1,2], and the bacterial concentrations are generally low in the early days [3]. Known as a prevailing foodborne pathogen, Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium (S. Typhimurium) can sicken or even kill an infected person within 12–72 h [4]. Classic detection strategies, such as cultures and immunological and nucleic acid-based techniques, are tied down by positives and high time costs [5,6]. Thus, quickly and correctly identifying foodborne pathogens is a critical issue for food safety.

Quantum dots (QDs) are relatively easy to synthesize, have highly tunable optical properties and have been widely used as optical markers [7,8]. Among all QDs, CDs possess many superiorities, such as good chemical stability, high photostability, remarkable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low preparation cost. Therefore, they have wide application prospects in light-emitting devices, fluorescence detection, photocatalysis, and biosensors [9,10]. Because the nitrogen (N) atomic radius is similar to the C atomic radius, N atoms easily mixed with the carbon skeleton material, and the amino groups on the surface of the CDs can enhance the conjugate degree of the conjugate system, increasing singlet electron transitions from the ground state to low excitation state, which is helpful for obtaining a higher fluorescence quantum yield [11]. Accordingly, N-doped CDs are the most studied and the most promising doping system [12–14]. Glowing fluorescent CDs are safer and more reliable for experiments in organisms and food. Despite this, individual CDs are prone to aggregation-induced quenching in the labeling process, and because CD particles are too small, it is difficult to isolate and purify CD-labeled aptamers [15]. Theoretically, integrating multiple QDs onto the surface or into the interior of a sphere is a good strategy for amplifying detectable signals to identify events [16]. CdSe/ZnSe/ZnS core/shell/shell QD nanobeads subjected to an oil-in-water emulsion-evaporation technique were used to amplify hepatitis B surface antigen protein detection signals as low as 78 pg [17]. In a recent study, masses of green CDs were stored in Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) cells for the immune detection of Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7 in milk, and the detection limit was 2.4 × 102 CFU/mL [18]. There is a risk of adding new contaminants to the sample if the CD carrier S. aureus cells are not completely inactivated.

To amplify the fluorescence signal without introducing new pollutants, we focused on the DNA nanostructure. DNA nanostructure is a special structure formed by DNA self-assembly through base complementary pairing [19,20]. Compared with unstructured DNA single strands, this nanostructure has a strict spatial structure and shape and is very stable. "Anchoring" CD to the vertices of the DNA structure amplifies the fluorescence signal steadily. Although aptamers have the same or even higher affinity than antibodies to targets obtained by traditional biological methods and are easier to synthesize and store [21–23], individual aptamers are relatively soft, resulting in them intertwining with each other in a liquid medium, which affects their stability and affinity due to the temperature, the substrate, and concentration or other external environment [24,25]. Tetrahedrons are remarkable DNA nanostructures formed by assembling four different nucleic acids, which overcome the disorderly fixation, interentanglement, and steric hindrance of single-stranded aptamers on the electrode surface [26,27]. This stable triangular cone probe shows higher anchoring stability and higher target accessibility than linear nucleic acids [28,29]. Given the above advantages, the DNA tetrahedron structure is a powerful tool for biosensors, drug delivery and cell communication. In this work, we designed the specific recognition of S. Typhimurium Td by projecting an S. typhimurium aptamer strand at one vertex. Td provides stable base support and distances aptamer chains from each other, avoiding entanglement and allowing better identification and grasping of targets.

Herein, we bound solid Td and CDs to obtain a novel nontoxic microsphere capable of signal amplification, in which Td serves as the skeleton and CD is the ‘joint’ emitting intense fluorescence under the specific binding action of streptavidin (SA) and desthiobiotin. In combination with magnetic beads (MBs), a sensitive sandwich aptsensor was established for the detection of S. typhimurium. We prepared two aptamers for S. Typhimurium protruding from the vertex of the tetrahedron (Td-Apt). One aptamer (Td-Apt1) was modified on the surface of the MBs to capture S. Typhimurium and isolate it from a complex matrix, and the other aptamer (Td-Apt2) was coupled with the prepared green nontoxic fluorescent CDs to form a fluorescent microsphere structure (Td-Apt2-CD421) that lit up the isolated S. Typhimurium. This is the first time this microsphere has been used for fluorescence signal amplification in the detection of foodborne pathogens. Our results demonstrate that the prepared microsphere structure can be used as a sensitive probe in pathogen detection.

The aptamers and biotin/desthiobiotin-modified oligonucleotides listed in Table S1 were synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co. (Shanghai, China). Biotin, SA, Tris, EDTA, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) were provided by Tianjin Macklin (Shanghai, China). SA-functionalized magnetic beads (SA-MBs) with a diameter of 2–3 µm were purchased from BaseLine ChromTech Research Center (Tianjin, China). DNA loading buffer (6×) and DNA markers were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system (Sartorius, Germany) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm. The detailed apparatus and characterization are described in the Supplementary materials.

S. Typhimurium (ATCC 14028), Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus, ATCC 17802), E. coli (ATCC 8739), and S. aureus (ATCC 6538) were purchased from the Guangdong Microbial Culture Collection Center (Guangzhou, China). In this work, E. coli, V. parahaemolyticus, and S. aureus served as nontarget bacteria. These four bacteria were stored at − 20 °C with 50% glycerol. First, they were cultured in LB medium (Aoboxing Biotech, Beijing, China) at 37 °C with shaking at 120 rpm. After incubation overnight, bacteria at a concentration of 101 ~ 108 CFU/mL were obtained by continuous 10-fold gradient dilution with sterile HEPES buffer. For enumeration of the bacteria, the bacterial samples were resuspended in sterile HEPES buffer solution, and 10 μL was absorbed and added to the blood cell counting board. The bacterial sample was counted with a microscope.

CD421 was synthesized as inspired by previous reports [30]. Briefly, 0.250 g of urea and 1.065 g of citric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and mixed evenly. The mixed liquid was transferred to a high-pressure reactor for water and heated to 180 °C for the hydration reaction. After 6 h, the reaction liquid was cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 48 h. The sample obtained after dialysis was the prepared N-doped CD421, and CD421 was stored at 4 ℃ away from light for later use. The detailed characterizations and ratio optimization of raw materials are described in the Supplementary materials.

The Td-Apt1 structure was prepared based on a previously reported method [26] as follows. The five oligonucleotides (strands Aptamer1, A, B-bio, C-bio, and D-bio) were dissolved in hybridization buffer (5 mM Tris-HCl, 12.5 mM MgCl2, and 12.5 mM NaCl; pH 7.4) mixed in stoichiometric equivalents, and the mixed concentration was controlled to 2 μM. The solution was heated to 95 °C for 10 min and then cooled to 4 °C for 30 min. The Td-Apt2 structure was synthesized in the same way by changing strands Aptamer1, A, B-bio, C-bio, and D-bio to Aptamer2, A, B-des, C-des, D-des, respectively.

Td-Apt1 was coupled onto SA-MB using the following method: Td-Apt1 was incubated with SA-MB at 37 ℃ for 30 min. Thereafter, the samples were magnetically separated, excess Td-Apt1 was removed, and then, the samples were re-suspended in 100 μL PBS buffer and stored at 4 ℃.

SA-CD421 was prepared based on the active ester method [31]. The prepared 20 μL CD421 was mixed evenly in PBS, and 1 mg/mL SA solution dissolved in PBS was added to it. Then, 30 μL of freshly prepared NHS and 20 μL of freshly prepared EDC were added to the mixed solution. After 2 h of incessant oscillation at RT, SA-functionalized CD421was obtained by filtration with a 0.2 µm PES membrane unit to remove the bound protein.

The preparation of Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres was based on the SA-desthiobiotin reaction. Desthiobiotin modified at three corners of Td can be combined with SA on CD421. At appropriate proportions, multiple Td-Apt2 and multiple SA-CD421 will form hollow microspheres with uniform size. SA-CD421 and the Td-Apt2 structure modified with desthiobiotin were mixed evenly at a molar ratio of 2:1 and oscillated. The reaction was allowed to sit for 30 min at 37 ℃, and then, the product was stored at 4 ℃ away from light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and establishment of the sandwich fluorescence detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Scheme 1, this strategy is based on the sandwich capture of two aptamers of the target. Td-Apt1-MBs captured target bacteria and separated the target and nontarget substances. The Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres function as a fluorescence signal amplifier. Td-Apt1-MBs and Td-Apt2-CD421 sandwich captured S. Typhimurium and amplify the signal through magnetic separation and biotin replacement by releasing a strong fluorescent signal. The constructed Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres involved two layers of signal amplification. The first layer of signal amplification is based on the supporting effect of Td-Apt on aptamer. The second layer of signal amplification occurs during biotin competition, which is based on the fact that the affinity between desthiobiotin and SA is weaker than that between biotin and SA (binding affinity of desthiobiotin is two orders of magnitude higher than that of biotin) [32]. In the presence of excess biotin, due to its stronger affinity, desthiobiotin is replaced by coupling SA on CD421, thereby releasing the CD421 concentrated on the target into PBS.

Two hundred microliters of Td-Apt1-MB and 400 μL of a 106 CFU/mL HEPES suspension of S. Typhimurium were mixed. After 60 min of incubation at 37 ℃, magnetic separation was carried out on the mixed sample for the first time to remove non-target substance. The precipitate was washed 3 times with PBS and then resuspended in 200 μL of Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres coupled with SA by desthiobiotin. Magnetic separation was carried out a second time after gentle oscillation at 37 ℃ for 30 min, thus the Td-Apt1-MB@S. Typhimurium@Td-Apt2-CD421 complex was isolated and the excess unbound Td-Apt2-CD421 was removed. The precipitate was rinsed 3 times with PBS and resuspended in 600 μL of 1 mM PBS solution containing biotin. Another 30 min of incubation at RT was performed, after which magnetic separation was carried out to isolate the Td-Apt1-MB@S. Typhimurium complex and Td-Apt2-CD421 eliminated after competition with biotin. The fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was positively correlated with the target concentration.

CD421 was synthesized through a one-step hydration method by citric acid and urea with a quantum yield of 10.31%. As shown by the UV/Vis result in Fig. 1a and b, the aqueous solution of CD421 had an obvious absorption peak at 332 nm and showed a blue color under a 365 nm hand-held UV lamp. The green and blue lines in Fig. 1b are the fluorescence spectra of CD421, revealing that CD421 has optimal excitation and emission peaks at 320 and 421 nm, and the photoluminescence of the prepared CD421 in this work is excitation-dependent, which is common in carbon materials [33] (Fig. 1a). CD421 in the AFM image in Fig. 1c was demonstrated to have good monodispersion. The height of CD421 was mostly less than 4.45 nm. The sizes of CD421s were also measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S1), and the average particle size was 2.94 nm. The size distribution of CD421 in the TEM analysis results is narrow (Fig. 1d and S1a are consistent with the TEM results). The dynamic light scattering (DLS) results revealed that the average hydrodynamic particle size of CD421 was 5.67 nm, as shown in Fig. S1c, indicating that CD421s was stable, with no aggregation, and the result was consistent with the particle size distribution statistics of TEM and AFM. The inset HRTEM image revealed that CD421 had a well-resolved lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene.

The assembly of Td-Apt was confirmed by 3% agarose gel electrophoresis and atomic force microscopy (AFM) [15,34]. For agarose gel electrophoresis results, images were visualized by an Image Lab software. As illustrated in Fig. 2a, the product stained with GelRed was analyzed, and lane 1 referred to the DNA ladder. With the addition of the DNA strands to lanes 2–6, the electrophoretic migration rate of DNA decreased markedly, and the migration of DNA in lane 6 was the slowest, which verified that Td-Apt was successfully synthesized as expected. According to the length of the four chains and the aptamer, we predicted that the resulting Td-Apt structure band would consist of approximately 200 bp. The three-dimensional structure of the tetrahedron caused the product to migrate at a lower rate, indicating that the Td-Apt product band in lane 6 was heavier than the pure double-chain DNA marker [35]. The assembly of Td-Apt was also characterized by AFM, as shown in Fig. 2b, and the length of a single Td-Apt were 12 nm. While the theoretical length of Td-Apt with a 17 bp side length is 5.78 nm, the discrepancy between the characterization result and the theoretical length is caused by the broadening effect, while the height reduction is caused by the extrusion of hollow three-dimensional nanostructures by the needle tip [36]. The detailed optimization process of Td-Apt synthesis is described in Supplementary materials Fig. S2.

We subsequently compared the affinities of Td-Apt and aptamers to S. Typhimurium by laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) and flow cytometry (FCM). The concentration of S. Typhimurium was 108 CFU/mL. The red spots in Fig. 2c and 2d represent S. Typhimurium captured by FAM-modified aptamers and Td-Apt, respectively. LSCM results showed that Td-Apt captured more target bacteria than the aptamer alone. The mean fluorescence intensity of the target after binding with FAM-modified Aptamer was 1727 while the fluorescence intensity of the target after binding with FAM-modified Td-Apt was 3357, which was 1.62-fold higher than that of aptamer (Fig. 2e). The result meant that Td-Apt had a stronger affinity for S. Typhimurium than the aptamer. This was because the rigid structure of Td supported the soft aptamer, and the edge of Td expanded the distance between each aptamer, which increased their difficulty to intertwine, avoiding the influence on target recognition due to the close distance [34].

MBs serve as magnetic separation carriers in this work. Td-Apt1-MNPs were prepared based on SA-biotin coupling. The Td-Apt1-functionalized MBs (Fig. S3a) were validated by zeta potential and absorption spectra changes. The zeta potentials of the MBs and Td-Apt1-MBs were measured, as shown in Fig. S3b. The zeta potential of Td-Apt1-MBs is − 14.67 mV, which reveals that coupling with plentiful Td changes the electric property of MNPs surface (6.24 mV). We further obtained the UV/Vis spectrum of Td-MB. After Td and MB were coupled, they were magnetically separated, and the UV/Vis spectra of the supernatant were measured (Fig. S3c). The Td solution had a characteristic peak at 260 nm, and the peaks of the supernatants after Td and MB coupling decreased in intensity at this wavelength as the amount of MB added was increased from 0 to 300 μL, indicating that no DNA remained in the supernatant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the formation of Td-Apt2-CD421, we performed AFM. As shown in Fig. 3a, the formation of Td-Apt2-CD421 presents a giant hollow sphere with a diameter of approximately 200 nm, confirming the formation of Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres. DLS results showed that the water and particle sizes of Td-Apt2-CD421 were mainly concentrated near 200 nm, much higher than those of Td (15.7 nm) and CD421 (5.67 nm) alone (Fig. S4a). The as-prepared Td-Apt2-CD421 solution became more electronegative when CD421 was coupled to Td (Fig. S4b). To achieve a higher yield, we optimized the amount of Td-Apt2 added to CD421 (Fig. S5). As the amount of Td-Apt2 increased, the extent of products accumulating at the target band increased gradually, and the maximum product band was obtained with an addition amount of 250 μL. In the formal experiment, 250 μL of Td-Apt2 was added to 10 μL of SA-functionalized CD421 (SA-CD421) to prepare Td-Apt2-CD421.

In order to ensure the biological safety of the experiment, the cytotoxicity of Td-Apt2-CD421s fluorescent microspheres was evaluated, and the evaluation results were shown in Fig. S6. The histogram corresponds to the absorbance of Td-Apt2-CD421s fluorescent microspheres at 450 nm within 0–4 days after the addition of cell culture medium and the blank control, and the line corresponds to the relative cell survival rate in the holes at 0–4 days compared with the blank control. As can be seen, from the second day of sample addition, the cell survival rate of Td-Apt2-CD421s fluorescent microspheres was significantly different from that of the control group. After three days of sample addition, the cell survival rate decreased to 85.88%. However, after four days of Td-Apt2-CD421s fluorescent microspheres, the cell survival rate of the sample group increased. It reached 94.17%, indicating that the biotoxicity of Td-Apt2-CD421s fluorescence microspheres prepared in this study is low, and it is expected to be applied in practical production and detection.

As a proof-of-concept experiment, we detected 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL S. Typhimurium by Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres. The blue line in Fig. 3b refers to the target detected by Td-Apt2-CD421, the red line represents S. Typhimurium detected by SA-CD421, and the gray line is the control test without S. Typhimurium in PBS buffer. Compared with the red line, the blue line has a strong characteristic fluorescence emission peak at 421 nm, while the control test has almost no fluorescence signal. These results verified that fluorescence signal amplification sandwich fluorescence detection platform based on Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescent microspheres is feasible.

To ascertain the fluorescence signal amplification efficiency of Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres, the target bacteria captured by single aptamer-CD421 and Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres were analyzed by LSCM and FCM. Fig. 4a-c reflect the difference between the fluorescence intensities of the target bacteria grabbed by the two materials. At the same concentration of S. Typhimurium to be measured (106 CFU/mL), targets captured by Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres (Fig. 4a right) emitted visibly stronger fluorescence signals than those capture by single aptamer-CD421 (Fig. 4a left). The histogram in Fig. 4b and c shows that the fluorescence intensity of S. Typhimurium captured by Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres after incubation was 2.33-fold that captured by single aptamer-CD421.

Unlike a single biotin-modified aptamer that can only be coupled to one CD421, the biotin modified on the three vertices of the Td structure can be coupled to several CD421s to form a sphere. There are many aptamers and fluorescent CD421s in the Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres. Once an aptamer in the microsphere recognizes the target bacteria, the fluorescence emitted by the CD421s in the entire microsphere will be much greater than that emitted by a single aptamer, CD421. Due to the close distance between CD421s in microspheres (the length of Td, <10 nm), a certain degree of self-fluorescence quenching was caused. After biotin competition, CD421s in the microspheres dropped and dispersed in the liquid system, and the distance between each CD421 increased; thus, the quenched fluorescence signal was released (Fig. 4d and e). The fluorescence intensity after biotin competition (biotin~SA-CD421, green) was 1.31-fold that of Td-CD421 microspheres (Td-CD421, blue). The values of the two signal magnifications were multiplied and we got the final signal magnification of 3.05 times.

The above results indicated that the fluorescence magnification of Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres was approximately 3.05-fold that of single aptamer-CD421. The magnification was smaller than expected, which may be due to fluorescence quenching inside the microspheres and fluorescence quenching between the microspheres at high concentrations [37,38].

The working parameters, such as the dose and concentration of MB and Td-Apt2-CD421, the recognition time and the biotin competition time, are important elements affecting the test performance. In this process, we used 1 × 108 CFU/mL S. Typhimurium. The dose of MB decided whether all the targets were brought to the final fluorescence detection step. As displayed in Fig. S7a, the fluorescence signal intensity and dose were positively correlated between 10 and 300 μL and no longer increased as the amount of MB increased. MB at 200 μL had a higher fluorescence intensity than the other amounts. Thus, 200 μL was selected for further experiments. Recognition time is another critical factor that can affect the sensitivity of the proposed fluorescence signal amplification assay. Fig. S7b shows that the responsive fluorescent signal continues to increase before 60 min and tends to be flat after 60 min. Hence, the optimal reaction time is 60 min.

The biotin competition time affects the conversion efficiency of CD421 in the Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres on S. Typhimurium into the fluorescent signal. The result (Fig. S6c) demonstrates that the responsive fluorescent signal continues to increase before 30 min and tends to be flat after, which indicates that all the Td-Apt2-CD421 microspheres disintegrated in the 30 min reaction time, and all the CD421s were dispersed in the solution. Hence, the optimal reaction time is 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the sandwich fluorescence assay
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal conditions, we determined the detection range and sensitivity of this proposed sandwich fluorescence amplification detection platform by detecting different concentrations of S. Typhimurium as the analyte. The fluorescence signals detected with varying concentrations of S. Typhimurium are illustrated in Fig. 5a. With increasing target bacteria concentration, the fluorescence signal was enhanced gradually. Plotting the logarithm of S. Typhimurium concentration as the abscissa, a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and cell concentration (with the blank and 10–108 CFU/mL) was observed, and the enhanced fluorescence intensity was correlated with the concentration of S. Typhimurium in the range of 10–108 CFU/mL. The linear equation is fluorescence intensity = 75.754ln(x) + 303.75, and the R2 value is 0.9941, with a detection limit for S. Typhimurium of 9 CFU/mL (calculated by 3 N/S).

We further investigated the specificity of this established assay against other common foodborne pathogens, including V. parahaemolyticus, E. coli, and S. aureus. The results in Fig. 5d show that significant fluorescence signals were detected only in the samples with S. Typhimurium added. In addition, the fluorescence intensity in other samples was not significantly different from that in the control.

To investigate the practicality of the proposed sandwich fluorescence amplification assay, we performed a recovery study in qualified pasteurized milk and drinking water samples. After the concentration of S. Typhimurium detected by this method was compared with the concentration of S. Typhimurium added (3.66 × 102, 3.66 × 104, and 3.66 × 106 CFU/mL), calculations showed that the capture efficiency ranged from 95.35–103.01% (Table S2). Several flushers during testing may be partly responsible for the loss.

In this study, we constructed an aptamer-guided fluorescence signal-amplifying biosensor for S. Typhimurium detection based on magnetic separation. We retrospectively reviewed the reported detection strategy in the last five years ( Table 1). Relative to other detection strategies, the proposed method was nontoxic and sensitive. Additionally, compared with other aptamers, this developed Td-Apt2-CD421 fluorescence signal amplification microsphere probe based on a DNA tetrahedron structure has the advantages of stronger affinity, higher sensitivity, easy synthesis, and simple modification. Combining with an efficient magnetic separation technique, this assay strategy offers simple manipulation and better specificity. In conclusion, this established fluorescence signal amplification sandwich biosensor could be sensitive enough to monitor S. Typhimurium residues quickly in water and food.

Wei Wu and Qingli Yang were responsible for the Conceptualization, funding acquisition and the original draft. Xu Zhang and Mingru Yao were responsible for the data analysis and manuscript typesetting. Han Du was responsible for method, data acquisition, curation, analysis, and manuscript writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Breeding Plan of Shandong Provincial Qingchuang Research Team (2019), Qingdao Science and Technology Fund (21-1-4-sf-6-nsh), the National Key Research and Development Program under Grant No. 2016YFD0400105, and High-level Talent Start-up fund from Qingdao Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131938.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Han Du is a doctoral student in Shandong Agricultural University. She got her bachelor degree from Shandong Agricultural University, master degree from North West Agriculture and Forestry University, and was enroll as a doctoral student in 2019. Her major is Food Science and Engineering, and research interests are biosensors based on nanomaterials and aptamer.
Xu Zhang is a doctoral student in College of Horticultural Science and Engineering, Shandong Agricultural University, majoring in Detection of plant viruses.
Mingru Yao is a postgraduate in College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, majoring in Food quality and safety.

Dr. Qingli Yang is a Professor at the Qingdao Agricultural University, China. He got his doctoral degree from Institute of Ocean, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research areas are focused on the molecule mechanism of mycotoxin generation and rapid detection of mycotoxin in food. He has published over 40 academic articles, yql@qau.edu.cn.

Dr. Wei Wu is a Professor at the Qingdao Agricultural University, China. She got her doctoral degree from Guangzhou Institutes of Biomedicine and Health, Chinese Academy of Sciences. After graduation, she joined Ocean University of China for her Post-doctor research. Her research areas are focused on new method or biosensors for food and environmental safety control, such as food-borne pathogens, toxins, heavy metals. She has published over 25 academic articles and one book, wuweiouc@126.com.
